days.title: Week 2, Day 8: Asking for Help

words.example_sentence_native: Excuse me, can you help me?

dialogues.context: Asking for help on the street.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Excuse me, can you help me?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Yes, of course. What is it?

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Sorry, ik begrijp het niet. Kunt u langzamer spreken?
lines.native: Sorry, I don't understand. Can you speak slower?
lines.phonetic: Sorry, ik be-chripe ut neet. Kunt u lang-zah-mer spray-ken?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Geen probleem. Ik zal het herhalen.
lines.native: No problem. I will repeat it.
lines.phonetic: Geen pro-bleem. Ik zal het her-hah-len.

dialogues.context: Feeling unwell and asking for help.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Help, ik voel me niet goed.
lines.native: Help, I don't feel well.
lines.phonetic: Help, ik fool me neet goot.

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Oké, ga zitten. Ik bel een ambulance.
lines.native: Okay, sit down. I will call an ambulance.
lines.phonetic: Okay, ga zit-ten. Ik bel un am-bu-lan-se.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Excuse me, what is this?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: That is a stroopwafel.

days.cultural_note: The Dutch are generally direct and willing to help, but it's always polite to start with 'Pardon' or 'Sorry'.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'g' sound in Dutch is a soft, throaty sound, similar to the 'ch' in the Scottish word 'loch'.

days.title: Week 2, Day 9: Understanding Questions

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Excuse me, how much does this cost?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: That is five euros.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: What is your name?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: My name is Jan.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Wanneer vertrekt de trein naar Amsterdam?
lines.native: When does the train to Amsterdam leave?
lines.phonetic: Wan-neer ver-trekt de trine naar Amsterdam?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: It leaves at ten o'clock.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Hello, how is it going?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Goed, dank u. En met u?
lines.native: Good, thank you. And with you?
lines.phonetic: Good, dank u. En met u?

days.cultural_note: Using the formal 'u' is safer when addressing strangers, especially older people. 'Je' is used for friends and family.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'ee' in 'Wie' and 'Hoeveel' is a long 'ay' sound, like in the English word 'say'.

days.title: Week 2, Day 10: Giving Simple Information

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Where do you come from?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: I come from Sri Lanka.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Do you speak English?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Yes, a little.

dialogues.context: When you don't know the answer.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Weet u hoe laat het is?
lines.native: Do you know what time it is?
lines.phonetic: Weet u hoo laat het is?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Sorry, I don't know.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Hallo, ik ben Peter. Wat is uw naam?
lines.native: Hello, I am Peter. What is your name?
lines.phonetic: Hallo, ik ben Peter. Wat is uw naam?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: My name is Fatima.

days.cultural_note: Dutch people appreciate it when you try to speak their language, even if it's just a few words.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'ij' in 'Mijn' sounds like the 'ay' in the English word 'my'.

days.title: Week 2, Day 11: Asking Location

words.native: Is there a bus stop here?

words.example_sentence_target: Waar kan ik een apotheek vinden?
words.example_sentence_native: Where can I find a pharmacy?
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Waar kan ik un a-po-take vin-den?

sentences.native: Excuse me, where is the station?
I am looking for a hotel.

sentences.target: Is er een bank in de buurt?
sentences.phonetic: Is er un bank in de buurt?

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Pardon, weet u waar het treinstation is?
lines.native: Excuse me, do you know where the train station is?
lines.phonetic: Par-don, weet u waar het trine-sta-shon is?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Ja, het is aan het einde van deze straat.
lines.native: Yes, it is at the end of this street.
lines.phonetic: Ya, het is aan het inde van day-ze straat.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Ik zoek de Albert Heijn supermarkt.
lines.native: I am looking for the Albert Heijn supermarket.
lines.phonetic: Ik zook de Albert Hine soo-per-markt.

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: That is around the corner.

dialogues.context: Asking for the nearest hospital in an emergency.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Help! Waar is het dichtstbijzijnde ziekenhuis?
lines.native: Help! Where is the nearest hospital?
lines.phonetic: Help! Waar is het dichtst-by-zy-nde zee-ken-hice?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Twee straten verderop. Ik zal u de weg wijzen.
lines.native: Two streets away. I will show you the way.
lines.phonetic: Tway strah-ten ver-der-op. Ik zal u de weg vy-zen.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Is hier een bushalte in de buurt?
lines.native: Is there a bus stop near here?
lines.phonetic: Is heer un bus-hal-te in de buurt?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Ja, daar is er een, tegenover de bakker.
lines.native: Yes, there is one over there, opposite the bakery.
lines.phonetic: Ya, daar is er een, tay-gen-oh-ver de bak-ker.

days.cultural_note: Many Dutch cities are very walkable or cyclable. Asking for directions is common and people are usually happy to point you in the right direction.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'ie' in 'ziekenhuis' is a long 'ee' sound, like in 'see'.

days.title: Week 2, Day 12: Directions

words.example_sentence_native: Turn left at the traffic lights.

words.example_sentence_target: De winkel is aan de rechterkant.
words.example_sentence_native: The store is on the right side.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: De win-kel is aan de rech-ter-kant.

sentences.target: Het is de tweede straat rechts.
sentences.native: It is the second street on the right.
sentences.phonetic: Het is de tway-de straat rechts.

sentences.target: Loop tot het einde van de straat.
sentences.native: Walk to the end of the street.
sentences.phonetic: Loop tot het inde van de straat.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Hoe kom ik bij het museum?
lines.native: How do I get to the museum?
lines.phonetic: Hoo kom ik by het mu-say-um?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Ga hier rechtdoor, en dan de eerste straat links. Het is aan uw rechterhand.
lines.native: Go straight ahead here, and then the first street left. It is on your right hand.
lines.phonetic: Ga heer recht-door, en dan de eer-ste straat links. Het is aan uw rech-ter-hand.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: So, right after the bridge?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Nee, vóór de brug rechts. Bij de stoplichten.
lines.native: No, right before the bridge. At the traffic lights.
lines.phonetic: Nay, voor de bruch rechts. By de stop-lich-ten.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Is it far away?

lines.speaker: B
lines.target: Nee, het is dichtbij. Ongeveer vijf minuten lopen.
lines.native: No, it is near. About a five-minute walk.
lines.phonetic: Nay, het is dicht-by. On-ge-veer vife min-oo-ten low-pen.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Can you stop here, please?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Of course. That will be 12 euros.
lines.phonetic: Na-tuur-luk. Dat is dan twaalf euro.

sentenceScramble.nativeHint: The second street on the right

days.cultural_note: Cyclists have right of way in many situations in the Netherlands. Always check for bike paths (fietspaden) before crossing a road.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'ui' sound in 'rechterhuis' is unique to Dutch. It's a bit like saying 'ow' in 'how' but with your lips rounded.

days.title: Week 2, Day 13: Public Places

words.example_sentence_target: Ik wil in een restaurant eten.
words.example_sentence_native: I want to eat in a restaurant.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Ik wil in un res-tow-rant ay-ten.

words.example_sentence_target: Zullen we een kopje koffie drinken in het café?
words.example_sentence_native: Shall we have a cup of coffee at the café?
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Zul-len we un kop-ye kof-fie drin-ken in het ca-fay?

words.example_sentence_target: Laten we in het park wandelen.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Lah-ten we in het park wan-de-len.

words.example_sentence_target: Ik ga naar de bibliotheek om een boek te lenen.
words.example_sentence_native: I'm going to the library to borrow a book.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Ik ga naar de bib-lee-o-take om un book te lay-nen.

words.example_sentence_native: On Saturday there is a market.

sentences.native: I am going to the market.

sentences.target: Het park is mooi in de lente.
sentences.native: The park is beautiful in the spring.
sentences.phonetic: Het park is moy in de len-te.

dialogues.context: Making plans to go to a restaurant.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Zullen we vanavond in een restaurant eten?
lines.native: Shall we eat at a restaurant tonight?
lines.phonetic: Zul-len we van-ah-vond in un res-tow-rant ay-ten?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Yes, good idea! Which restaurant?

dialogues.context: At the entrance of a museum.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Twee kaartjes voor het museum, alstublieft.
lines.native: Two tickets for the museum, please.
lines.phonetic: Tway kaart-yes voor het mu-say-um, al-stu-bleeft.

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: That will be thirty euros.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: When does the library close?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: At five o'clock.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: Two coffees, please.

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Coming right up.

sentenceScramble.nativeHint: On Saturday there is a market

days.cultural_note: A Dutch 'café' is often more like a pub or bar (a 'brown cafe' or 'bruin café') where people drink beer and socialize, not just coffee.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'a' in 'Markt' is a short sound, like the 'a' in 'car'.

days.title: Week 2, Day 14: Travel

words.example_sentence_target: Ik neem de trein naar Rotterdam.
words.example_sentence_native: I am taking the train to Rotterdam.
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Ik name de trine naar Rotterdam.

words.example_sentence_target: Welke bus gaat naar het centrum?
words.example_sentence_native: Which bus goes to the city center?
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Wel-ke bus gaat naar het sen-trum?

words.example_sentence_native: The airplane is departing on time.
I need to buy a ticket.

words.example_sentence_target: Hoe kom ik op het vliegveld?
words.example_sentence_native: How do I get to the airport?
words.example_sentence_phonetic: Hoo kom ik op het fleech-veld?

sentences.target: Vanaf welk spoor vertrekt de trein?
sentences.native: From which platform does the train leave?
sentences.phonetic: Vanaf welk spoor ver-trekt de trine?

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Goedemorgen. Eén kaartje naar Den Haag, alstublieft.
lines.native: Good morning. One ticket to The Hague, please.
lines.phonetic: Goo-de-mor-gen. Ayn kaart-ye naar Den Haag, al-stu-bleeft.

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Single trip or return?

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Pardon, gaat deze bus naar het centraal station?
lines.native: Excuse me, does this bus go to the central station?
lines.phonetic: Par-don, gaat day-ze bus naar het sen-traal sta-shon?

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: No, you need to have bus 22.
lines.phonetic: Nay, u moot bus tway-en-twin-tig heb-ben.

lines.speaker: A
lines.target: Ik wil graag inchecken voor de vlucht naar Londen.
lines.native: I would like to check in for the flight to London.
lines.phonetic: Ik wil graag in-check-en voor de vlucht naar London.

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: May I see your passport?

dialogues.context: In a taxi to the airport.

lines.speaker: A
lines.native: To Schiphol airport, please.

lines.speaker: B
lines.native: Okay, no problem.

days.cultural_note: The OV-chipkaart is a rechargeable smart card for all public transport in the Netherlands. It's very convenient if you plan to travel frequently.
days.pronunciation_tip: The 'ei' in 'reizen' and 'trein' has the same sound as 'ij', like the 'ay' in 'my'.
